name: Acton Lead Mines
short_description: Historic lead mining site in southern Maine offering rockhounding opportunities in a gentle sloped terrain. Search for quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens from the old workings.
description:
Acton Lead Mines represents one of Maine's early industrial heritage sites in York County. The property features abandoned lead mine workings where collectors can search for mineral specimens associated with historic ore processing. The site sits within the New England Uplands geology, where Paleozoic metamorphic rocks host veins and pockets enriched with quartz and feldspar minerals.

The gentle slope terrain makes this location accessible for collectors of varying skill levels. Visitors can explore the old mining areas and surrounding rock outcrops to locate mineral specimens. The quartz found here often occurs as clear to milky crystals, while feldspar specimens may display the characteristic luster and cleavage patterns typical of Maine's metamorphic assemblages.

This is a historical site with educational value beyond just specimen collection. The old mine workings provide insight into Maine's 18th and 19th century mining operations. Rockhounders should respect any safety barriers around unstable shaft areas while focusing their collecting efforts on safe surface outcrops and tailings piles.

The location is best visited during Maine's spring through fall season when weather is mild and trails are passable. The proximity to Acton makes it a convenient stop for those exploring southern Maine's rockhounding opportunities. Always check local property access conditions before visiting, as historic mine sites may have seasonal closures or access restrictions.
history: The Acton Lead Mines developed during the late 1700s and 1800s as part of Maine's early mineral extraction industry, operating within metamorphic bedrock formed during the Paleozoic Acadian mountain-building event. The quartz and feldspar found here are primary minerals of the host metamorphic rocks that were altered by hydrothermal fluids associated with the lead ore mineralization.
tips: Search the tailings piles and loose rock around the old mine entrances for the best quartz and feldspar specimens - these areas concentrate weathered mineral fragments. Look for translucent to opaque quartz crystals with a glassy luster and examine feldspar pieces for the characteristic striations (parallel lines) on cleavage faces. Bring a small hammer or pick to carefully expose fresh mineral surfaces on exposed rock outcrops, and use a hand lens to identify the distinctive flat cleavage planes of feldspar versus the irregular fracture of quartz.
terrain_notes: Elevation 696 feet on gently sloping terrain with mixed woodland vegetation typical of southern Maine. Walking distances to active outcrop areas are generally short from parking, with footing ranging from stable grass to loose talus and mine rubble. No steep climbs required, but watch for uneven mine tailings and scattered rocks underfoot.
best_season: May - October. Late spring through fall offers the most favorable weather conditions in Maine. Summer (June-August) provides the longest daylight and most stable ground conditions. Avoid winter months when snow and ice make the site hazardous and difficult to access.
written_directions: From Acton, head east on Route 109 for approximately 1.5 miles. The Acton Lead Mines site is located on private or historically protected land near the coordinates 43.483159, -70.9104. Look for historic markers or mine entrance signage along the roadside. Note: Confirm current access status with local Acton town offices before visiting, as historic mine sites may have restricted hours or seasonal closures.
road_conditions: Paved town roads (Route 109) provide access. Parking area conditions depend on current site management and maintenance.
parking_notes: Limited roadside parking near site entrance. Capacity likely 2-4 vehicles. Parking details should be confirmed with Acton town offices.
permit_link: Contact Acton Town Offices to confirm access permission and any required permits for rockhounding at this historic mine site.
collection_rules: Permission from site owners or managers is required. Personal use collection only. Respect all safety barriers around unstable mine shafts and entrances. No motorized equipment or excavation. No commercial mineral collection.
rules:
Obtain permission from site managers before collecting
Personal use only - no commercial collection
Respect all safety barriers and mine shaft closures
No motorized digging or heavy equipment
Collect only from safe surface outcrops and tailings areas
Leave no trash
nearest_services: Acton, Maine - approximately 1-2 miles. Basic services including general store and limited dining options. Larger services available in nearby Bridgton (10 miles north) and Sanford (15 miles south).
accessibility_notes: Gentle sloping terrain with relatively easy walking and minimal elevation gain makes this location accessible to most hikers and families. Footwork ranges from stable grass to loose mine rubble requiring careful stepping. Not suitable for wheelchair access due to natural terrain and mine rubble. Sturdy footwear recommended.

faq.question: What minerals can you find rockhounding at Acton Lead Mines in Maine?
faq.answer: The primary minerals found at Acton Lead Mines are quartz, feldspar, and mica. Quartz occurs as clear to milky crystals with a glassy luster, while feldspar displays the characteristic striations and cleavage patterns typical of Maine's metamorphic rocks. These minerals are found in the tailings piles and exposed outcrops around the historic mine workings.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Acton Lead Mines free or do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Access to Acton Lead Mines requires permission from site managers or owners, as this is a private historic property. Contact the Acton Town Offices to confirm current access conditions and any required permits before visiting. There is no standard public rockhounding fee, but access permission is mandatory.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at the Acton Lead Mines?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding season at Acton Lead Mines is May through October. Late spring through early fall offers the most favorable Maine weather conditions, with June through August providing the longest daylight and most stable ground conditions. Avoid winter months when snow and ice create hazards and access difficulties.

faq.question: Are the Acton Lead Mines suitable for beginner rockhounders and families?
faq.answer: Yes, the Acton Lead Mines are beginner-friendly and suitable for families with children ages 8 and up. The gentle sloping terrain requires minimal hiking effort, and mineral specimens like quartz and feldspar are relatively easy to identify. However, always supervise children around historic mine areas and respect all safety barriers marking unstable shafts.

faq.question: What tools and safety precautions do I need for rockhounding at Acton Lead Mines?
faq.answer: Bring a small hammer or pick, hand lens, and sturdy footwear with good grip for uneven mine tailings. Wear long pants and gloves to protect against sharp mine rubble and quartz fragments. Most importantly, stay clear of all mine shaft entrances and respect posted safety barriers. Never enter abandoned shafts or enclosed mine workings.

faq.question: How far is Acton Lead Mines from major towns and what services are nearby?
faq.answer: Acton Lead Mines is located in the town of Acton in York County, Maine, approximately 1-2 miles from town center. Acton offers basic services including a general store. For more extensive amenities, Bridgton is about 10 miles north and Sanford is approximately 15 miles south, both offering gas stations, restaurants, and lodging options.
